body:
Tried to reproduce on 1.24.2 — rebuilt from source, ran `su root` and `su - root` in a fresh `archlinux:latest` container, both render `root` with `style_root` applied:

```
=== alice → su root (no -) ===
Password: 0          # id -u
[1;31mroot[0m in     # starship module username
```

`geteuid()` returns 0 after `su` and `whoami::username()` returns `"root"`, so `is_root` is true and the style maps to `style_root`. Same result with the default-features `show_always = false` (omitted here for the diagnostic — forced with `show_always = true` yields identical output).

A few commands from the actually-affected shell would help narrow it:

```
# after `su` or `su root` on the host seeing the issue:
id -u
whoami
starship module username
```

If `id -u` is non-zero after su on your system, euid isn't actually elevating (rootless container, user namespace, or a non-default PAM stack could cause that). If `id -u` is 0 but `starship module username` still renders with `style_user`, that would be a reproducible bug at the module level and useful to share.

Thank you for getting back to me. These are the results I'm getting after doing `su` (which asks for the root password).

```
 id -u
0

 whoami
root

 starship module username
root in

```

Nonetheless, the username displayed in the prompt is that of my regular unelevated user. And the font color is "text" not "red".

Helpful counter-repro. The module renders `root` in isolation but the prompt doesn't, which narrows the bug away from the username module's `is_root()` check and toward whatever is composing the prompt.

Three pieces to triangulate:

1. The `[username]` and `[palettes.*]` sections of your `starship.toml`. `text` and `red` read like palette names, so your config may be overriding the module's default `style_root` / `style_user` mapping.
2. Shell + version (`bash --version` / `zsh --version` / etc.) and how starship is loaded (the line(s) sourcing the init in your rc file).
3. Output of `STARSHIP_LOG=trace starship prompt 2>&1 | grep -iE 'username|user|root|euid|module'` after `su`. The trace will show whether the username module is firing in the full prompt path with `is_root=true` like it does in isolation, or whether the prompt-composition step is reaching for env vars instead.

The contradiction between `starship module username` working manually and the prompt showing your unelevated user is the wedge. One of those three will say where the divergence is.

Thank you for all your efforts to get to the bottom of this!

1. I removed `starship.toml` and replaced it with a file that contains nothing but `format = '$all'` (which is defined as the default on starship.rs/config).

2. `bash --version` returns `GNU bash, version 5.3.9(1)-release (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)`
The relevant line in `~/.bashrc` reads like this: `eval "$(starship init bash)"`

3. Using the above mentioned rudimentary default starship config file, doing `su` still does not change the prompt. The prompt looks like this:

```
~ 
❯ su
Password: 

~ 
❯
```

Colors are mint (?) and green. They don't change with the change to elevated user.

`STARSHIP_LOG=trace starship prompt 2>&1 | grep -iE 'username|user|root|euid|module'` right after doing `su` returns the following:

```

\[\]root\[\] in \[\]/home/username\[\]
```

Curiously, in the very last line printed above, `\]root\[` is printed in red and `\]/home/username\[` is in mint (?) color.

The trace is the wedge. Three things from it:

1. Manual `starship prompt` after `su` renders `\[\]root\[\] in \[\]/home/username\[\]` — username module IS firing with `is_root=true` and `style_root` (red).
2. starship's home_dir = `/root` (it uses `getpwuid(geteuid())->pw_dir`, not `$HOME`), so it reads config from `/root/.config/starship.toml`.
3. Your live prompt is `~ \n ❯` — directory collapsed to `~`, no username module visible.

Those two prompts come from the same starship binary but render differently. That's only possible if (a) starship in the live prompt is running with different args/config than your manual call, or (b) the live prompt isn't going through starship at all in the post-`su` shell.

`su` without `-` starts a new interactive bash that sources `/root/.bashrc` (root's, not yours). If root's `.bashrc` doesn't run `eval "$(starship init bash)"`, the new shell has no `starship_precmd` in `PROMPT_COMMAND`, and whatever PS1 root's profile sets renders instead. A hardcoded `PS1='~\n❯ '` in root's shell init would explain `~ \n ❯` exactly — including the "doesn't change with euid" behavior, since that PS1 has no logic in it.

Three quick diagnostics in the post-`su` shell:

```
echo "$PROMPT_COMMAND"
declare -p PS1
type starship_precmd
```

If `PROMPT_COMMAND` is empty or doesn't reference starship, that's the bug — root's shell isn't initializing starship, so the manual `starship prompt` works but PROMPT_COMMAND never fires it. Fix is one line in `/root/.bashrc`:

```
eval "$(starship init bash)"
```

If `PROMPT_COMMAND` does call starship and `starship_precmd` exists as a function, then we're back to a real bug and it'd be useful to see the exact `starship_precmd` body — the args it passes might differ from a bare `starship prompt`.

Now I feel pretty stupid. :S All the more thanks to you for helping me find what seems to have been a silly mistake on my side.

I ran `type starship_precmd` and got `bash: type: starship_precmd: not found`.

Putting `eval "$(starship init bash)"` in `/root/.bashrc` and copying `/home/user/.config/starship.toml` to `/root/.config/` solved my issue. It's all working perfectly now. I guess I should have thought of that myself. I just assumed that starship would continue governing the prompt even after user elevation given the existence of the option `style_root`, but I get how it works now.

Maybe it should be added to the configuration guide for people like me. *covers their face*

Anyway, again, big thanks to you!

Not stupid at all — `style_root` reads like a global setting, so assuming starship would keep running after `su` is a reasonable inference. The init line is per-shell process, though, so anything that opens a fresh login shell (`su`, `sudo -i`, `doas`) needs it sourced again on that side.

Glad it's working. Sending a small docs PR to call this out near the username module.
